1: Chapter 45 is temporally displaced: bašššānâ hārĕbîʿît lîhôyāqîm ben-Yōšiyyāhû — the fourth year of Jehoiakim (605 BC), when Baruch wrote Jeremiah's dictated scroll. This is the same year as the scroll incident in Jeremiah 36. Placed after the Egypt narrative (Jeremiah 43-44), this oracle functions as a retrospective word to Baruch — a word that applies to his entire ministerial career alongside Jeremiah. The temporal displacement is intentional: the oracle to Baruch closes the prose biographical section before the oracles against the nations begin in Jeremiah 46.
2: kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾēlêkā Bārûk — the divine oracle addressed personally to Baruch. Throughout Jeremiah, Baruch appears as the indispensable but unnamed collaborator — secretary, reader, witness. Here YHWH addresses him directly, acknowledging his individual existence before the covenant God. The address is intimate: ʾēlêkā (to you) — to you specifically, Baruch.
3: ʾāmartā ʾôy-nāʾ lî kî-yāsap YHWH yāgôn ʿal-maḵʾōbî — "You said: Woe is me! For the LORD has added sorrow to my pain. I am weary with my groaning, and I find no rest." Baruch's lament is quoted back to him by YHWH — YHWH has been listening to Baruch's private complaint. Yāgôn ʿal-maḵʾōbî (sorrow added to my pain) — the accumulation: pain already exists and YHWH adds more. The MKT renders the lament in first person to preserve the emotional directness of Baruch's complaint.
4: kōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnēh ʾăšer-bānîtî ʾănî hōrēs — "Thus says the LORD: What I have built I am tearing down, and what I have planted I am uprooting — this entire land." The vocabulary of Jeremiah's commissioning (Jeremiah 1:10: linṯôš wĕlinthōs ûlĕhaʾăbîd ûlĕharōs ûlibnet ûlintôaʿ) is recycled in a cosmic scope: what YHWH himself built, YHWH himself tears down. Baruch's personal suffering is contextualized within the universal divine action: the whole land is being unmade. Personal sorrow is real, but it is proportionate to a cosmically significant moment.
5: wĕʾattâ tĕbaqqēš-lĕkā gĕdōlôt ʾal-tĕbaqqēš — "And you — are you seeking great things for yourself? Stop seeking them." The rebuke is gentle but clear: Baruch's lament conceals an expectation of personal greatness or comfort that the current moment cannot provide. Kî hinnĕnî mēbîʾ rāʿâ ʿal-kol-bāśār — "For I am going to bring disaster on all flesh" — the scope of the judgment is universal; Baruch's personal ambitions must be surrendered to this reality. The final promise: ûnātattî lĕkā ʾet-napšĕkā lĕšālāl ʾal-kol-hamĕqōmôt ʾăšer tēlēk šām — "but I will give you your life as a prize of war in all the places where you go." Nepeš lĕšālāl (life as plunder/prize) — life itself, not achievement or recognition, is the promised gift. The MKT renders "your life as a prize of war" preserving the military metaphor: survival when others fall is itself the victory.
